title: Deep Q-Network — Atari Breakout
summary: CNN Q-approximator -> replay buffer + target network -> Huber + Adam
description: A convolutional DQN trained from raw Atari pixels. The Q-table is replaced by a 3-conv / 2-fc network that maps a (4, 84, 84) stack of grayscale frames to Q-values over the 4 Breakout actions. A 100k-capacity replay buffer decorrelates samples and a frozen target network (synced every 1000 steps) stabilises the bootstrap target. Trained 3000 episodes on CPU.

demo.caption: Evaluation game from the episode-2900 checkpoint (ε=0.05). Autoplays muted, loops.

learning_method.title: The update rule
learning_method.formula: Q(s, a; θ) <- Q(s, a; θ) + α * [r + γ * max_a' Q_target(s', a'; θ⁻) - Q(s, a; θ)]
points: Q(s, a; θ) is now a neural network — a single forward pass outputs Q-values for every action
Q_target(s', a'; θ⁻) is a FROZEN copy of the network, updated every 1000 steps — stops the bootstrap target from shifting underneath the regression
transitions are stored in a replay buffer; each training step samples a random batch of 32 — breaks temporal correlation and lets every step be re-used
loss is smooth-L1 (Huber) — quadratic for small TD errors, linear for large ones, so outlier rewards don't explode the gradient
on terminal transitions the (1 - done) factor zeroes out the future term, same pattern as the tabular projects

what_i_learned: Function approximation replaces the Q-table once the state space breaks enumeration — the network learns a Q-FUNCTION, not a lookup, so visually similar states share structure automatically.
A replay buffer is the cost of moving away from tabular: in-place updates over correlated transitions diverge with a neural net, so you random-sample from stored experience instead.
A target network is the other cost: bootstrapping `max Q(s', a')` off the network being updated chases a moving target. Freeze a copy, update it every N steps.
Frame stacking is how the network sees velocity — a single frame hides ball direction, so 4 channel-stacked frames give it the temporal context it can't otherwise infer.
Huber loss over MSE matters when TD errors can be huge — MSE's quadratic tail blows up on outliers, Huber caps the gradient magnitude.
things_to_try_next: Double DQN — decouple action selection (online net) from action evaluation (target net) to cut the max-bias overestimation of standard DQN.
Prioritised replay — sample high-TD-error transitions more often; uniform sampling wastes capacity on already-solved states.
Dueling heads — split the output into V(s) and A(s, a) streams; useful in Breakout where most actions are equivalent on most frames.
Longer training on GPU — 3k episodes on CPU is a proof of concept; the emergent tunnelling strategy needs ~10x more experience.
